title: Nominative Pronouns
text: Approximate time: 30 minutes

title: Nominative Pronouns
text:
Aren’t these just pronouns?
While reading this you might be asking yourself, “Aren’t these just pronouns?” and “What does it mean to specifically be a nominative pronoun?” These are great questions and it is a distinction that we will be making early in these lessons. Let’s consider this sentence in English:

He eats her.

He and her are both pronouns in the above sentence, but He is the subject of the sentence while her is the thing that the verb is acting upon. We don’t say in English:

He eats she.

As you can see in English, our pronouns sometimes change based upon whether we are the doer of the action or the receiver of the action. This difference exists in German as well and is more nuanced. We will discuss this much more in later sections, but this is just to acknowledge that this difference exists so that we can demonstrate that not all pronouns are nominative, even in English.

title: Nominative Pronouns
section: What do we mean by formal and informal?
text:
What do we mean by formal and informal?
In German, when you are addressing certain people, you will use the formal version of you and other times you will use the informal version. Times you would consider using the formal version of you would be:

Speaking to someone you’ve never meet before
Speaking to someone of authority (police, teachers, etc.)
Speaking to someone significantly older than you as a sign of respect

The general rule that I use is:






NoteWhen to use the formal version of You



If a Southerner would use Sir or Ma’am to address this person, you’re probably using the formal version.



Moving from formal to informal
Some people, like police officers, you will always address with the formal version of You. However, like using Sir or Ma’am, someone may say something like “You don’t need to use Sir/Ma’am with me” and Germans have this same concept, but it is a bit more subtle. The person that commands respect will use the informal du to address you. At which point, you are now in a informal you relationship with this person for the rest of your life. The general rule for this is:






NoteWhen to transition from formal to informal



Once they use the informal version to address you, then you are now on an informal basis with this person for the rest of your life.




Differentiating between Sie and sie
You might notice from the above table that Sie (formal version of you (singular and plural)) is spelled the same as sie (third-person singular she and plural they). The pronunciation of these is the exact same and requires context to differentiate when speaking. The conjugation of the verb that follows can help with this context.
In written form the major distinction is that the the capitalized Sie refers to the formal version while the lowercase sie refers to either she or they. However, words at the start of a sentence are capitalized like in English and given that these are nominative pronouns, they are usually the subject of the sentence. As a result, it is common to see sie referring to she or they capitalized, but only because they start a sentence and not because they refer to the formal you.


Formal usage moving forward
We will limit the use of the formal you for simplicity moving forward in these lessons, but I will note it were applicable. This decision is to try to streamline examples and because, from my experience, learners oftentimes get a fair bit of latitude on this distinction. German speakers are generally excited to see you trying to speak German and not as focused on these social nuances. That being said, if I ran into a problem and needed help from the police or was meeting my partner’s parents/grandparents for the first time, I would try to give extra special attention toward using the formal version.






TipExercise



Make flashcards for these nominative pronouns and practice them until you feel confident with them.

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
text: Approximate time: 45 minutes

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
text:
Infinitive stems ending in -s, -ß, -x or -z
Infinitive verbs whose stem ends in -s, -ß, -x or -z only add a -t in the second-person singular. The rationale for this is that these infinitive stems already have either an -s sound at the end of the stem or a sound that is somewhat similar to -s. As a result, adding an -st ending wouldn’t be heard any different than -t. Some common words that fall into this category are:









-s
-ß
-x
-z




reisen 🔊 (to travel)
heißen 🔊 (to be named)
faxen 🔊 (to fax)
tanzen 🔊 (to dance)



schließen 🔊 (to close)
mixen 🔊 (to mix)
sitzen 🔊 (to sit)



grüßen 🔊 (to greet)

besitzen 🔊 (to own)









NoteRelation between -ß and -ss



This is probably a good place to point out that -s and -ß related. The -ß is sometimes replaced with -ss. As such, rules that apply to -s will also apply to -ß.


Let’s see an example of one of these conjugations:

heißen 🔊 (to be named)








ich heiße 🔊
wir heißen 🔊


du heißt 🔊
ihr heißt 🔊


er heißt 🔊sie heißtes heißt
Sie heißen 🔊sie heißen

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
text:
Infinitive stems ending in -d or -t
Infinitive stems ending in -d or -t need to have an -e added after the infinitive stem but prior to the -st or -t in order to be able to pronounce it. Some common words that fall into this category are:







-d
-t




baden 🔊 (to bathe)
arbeiten 🔊 (to work)


schneiden 🔊 (to cut)
antworten 🔊 (to answer)



retten 🔊 (to rescue)



beten 🔊 (to pray)



Let’s see this conjugation in practice:

baden 🔊 (to bathe)








ich bade 🔊
wir baden 🔊


du badest 🔊
ihr badet 🔊


er badet 🔊 sie badet es badet
Sie baden 🔊 sie baden




arbeiten 🔊 (to work)








ich arbeite 🔊
wir arbeiten 🔊


du arbeitest 🔊
ihr arbeitet 🔊


er arbeitet 🔊 sie arbeitetes arbeitet
Sie arbeiten 🔊 sie arbeiten

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
section: Infinitive stems ending in -m or -n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r
text:
Infinitive stems ending in -m or -n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r
Additionally, if the verb stem ends in an -m or -n that is preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r then we need to add an -e, just like the circumstances of infinitive stems ending with -d or -t above.
This rule can be a bit difficult for learners because it matters how one defines a “consonant sound”. Does the sound before the -m or -n sound like a vowel, -l or r? If so, it is treated normally. Otherwise, we need to add an -e. Let’s look at common application of this rule, then look at examples where we need to think about this “consonant sound” idea.

atmen 🔊 (to breathe)








ich atme 🔊
wir atmen 🔊


du atmest 🔊
ihr atmet 🔊


er atmet 🔊sie atmetes atmet
Sie atmen 🔊sie atmen




öffnen 🔊 (to open)








ich öffne 🔊
wir öffnen 🔊


du öffnest 🔊
ihr öffnet 🔊


er öffnet 🔊sie öffnetes öffnet
Sie öffnen 🔊sie öffnen



A table of common verbs that fall into the category of requiring an -e to be added are below:







-m preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r
-n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r




atmen 🔊 (to breathe)
öffnen 🔊 (to open)



rechnen 🔊 (to calculate)



zeichnen 🔊 (to draw)



trocknen 🔊 (to dry (something))









NoteComment on the frequency of this category of verbs



There aren’t a ton of verbs that fall into this category. In fact, for the category of having an -m preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r, atmen 🔊, and it’s derivatives (einatmen 🔊 (to breathe in), ausatmen 🔊 (to breathe out), durchatmen 🔊 (to catch your breath), aufatmen 🔊 (to breathe a sigh of relief), etc.) is the only verb (that I am aware of) that falls into this category.
For infinitive stems with -n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r, it is also quite limited. The table above covers all of the most common ones and a handful of others do exist (begegnen 🔊 (to meet (by chance)), entgegnen 🔊 (to reply (often contradictory)), segnen 🔊 (to bless), etc.), but they are quite infrequent to come across as a beginner.








NoteInfinitive stems ending in an -m or -n that are preceded by vowel sounds, -l or -r do not add an -e to the infinitive stem



To prove the rule, let’s look at a few infinitive stems that either end in -m or -n and are preceded by a vowel sound, -l or -r.

lernen 🔊 (to learn)








ich lerne 🔊
wir lernen 🔊


du lernst 🔊
ihr lernt 🔊


er lernt 🔊sie lerntes lernt
Sie lernen 🔊sie lernen




wärmen 🔊 (to warm)








ich wärme 🔊
wir wärmen 🔊


du wärmst 🔊
ihr wärmt 🔊


er wärmt 🔊sie wärmtes wärmt
Sie wärmen 🔊sie wärmen



Unlike the infinitive stems ending in -m or -n preceded by a consonant sound other than l or r, these are relatively more common. I have provided a list below of some of the more common ones you might come across:



beginnen 🔊 (to begin)
sich besinnen 🔊 (to recollect / to think better of)
brennen 🔊 (to burn)
dienen 🔊 (to serve (a cause))
entfernen 🔊 (to remove (something))
erwähnen 🔊 (to mention)
filmen 🔊 (to film)



sich gewöhnen* 🔊 (to become accustomed)
kennenlernen 🔊 (to get to know/to meet)
kommen 🔊 (to come)
lernen 🔊 (to learn)
meinen 🔊 (to mean)
wärmen 🔊 (to warm)
wohnen* 🔊 (to reside (somewhere))



* Note in these examples that the -n is preceded by an -h and you may be inclined to think that these are breaking the rule. However, in this context, the sound prior to the -n still has a “vowel-like” sound. This is part of the reason why this rule is the tricky.
I am also not aware of any circumstances where the infinitive stem has a -ln pattern and filmen is the only one I know with the -lm pattern.

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Regular
text:
Approximate time: 30 minutes

Learning Objectives
In this lesson, the learner will:

Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense



Conjugating verbs in the Present tense
Conjugation is how a verb changes based upon the doer of the action. In English, this looks like:

I jump
She jumps

The first-person singular version of “to jump” is jump, while the third-person singular version is jumps. In English, conjugation is usually pretty straightforward in many tenses. So much so, that we forget that we do it. However, many languages, including German, have more complexity in their conjugation and different subjects have different conjugations.
Let’s demonstrate conjugation of a regular verb in German, gehen, which means “to go”:







ich gehe
wir gehen


du gehst
ihr geht


er gehtsie gehtes geht
Sie* gehensie gehen



* Note that the formal Sie follows the conjugation of the third-person, plural, even though it refers to the second-person singular or plural. This will be true in all tenses.
For regular verbs the rule is to remove the -en ending from the infinitive version of the verb and add these endings for a given subject:



-e
-en


-st
-t


-t
-en









TipExercise



Let’s introduce a few more common regular verbs in the present tense.



Deutsch
English




machen
to do or to make


spielen
to play


lernen
to learn


fragen
to ask


sagen
to say


kaufen
to buy




Create flashcards to learn these common verbs
Conjugate each of the verbs

Code

Description
The goal of these materials are to provide a basic understanding of the German language for English-speakers where the explanations for the concepts and sentence structure will be provided in English. I should be clear from the start that my grasp of the German language is incomplete, but I am trying to write up lessons as my knowledge grows.
A major concept for language learning is vocabulary acquisition. In these materials, we are going to attempt to limit the vocabulary and rather focus on how the language itself works. At some points vocabulary will be necessary to progress, but I will be making attempts to minimize this as much as possible.

Learning Objectives
In these lessons, the learner will:

Describe grammar concepts from German
Assemble German sentences

A Note about General Rules
Throughout the materials we will be using “General Rules”. These are rules that hold in many, if not all, circumstances. We will denote them but putting them in a special block like:






NoteHow “General Rules” will look



“General Rules” will be in special blocks like this.

title: Modal Verbs
text: Approximate time: 30 minutes

title: Modal Verbs
text:
The second verb is implied
Sometimes we don’t use a second verb in a sentence with a modal verb, because the second verb is implied from the context. This idea exists in English as well:

Do you have to work?
Yes, I have to.

In this case, we see from the context of the sentence that “Yes, I have to.” is referring to having to work. Without that context, this sentence doesn’t really make sense in English. Additionally, this sentence is just abbreviating the longer sentence “Yes, I have to work.”.






NoteThis process has a special name



The second verbs in these sentences are elliptically omitted. This means that these words are absent, but the reader/listener can understand and “fill-in” given the context.


This same idea exists in German as well and we can use that same example sentence to demonstrate it:

Musst du arbeiten?
Ja, ich muss.

In this case, “arbeiten” is implied from the context and so it doesn’t need to be repeated, but, like in English, you could include it.

title: Modal Verbs
section: Discussing the desire for an object
text:
Discussing the desire for an object
When the modal verb is discussing the desire for an object, it is technically no longer acting as a modal verb. Remember earlier that we defined modal verbs as combining with an additional verb to describe the speaker’s attitude towards an action. Since we are no longer describing an attitude towards an action, but rather towards an object, the verbs are no longer acting as modal verbs. They are simply acting as normal verbs (sometimes referred to as lexical or full verbs). In other words, modal verbs can also be used in a non-modal context. This situation often arises when using the verbs mögen (to like) and wollen (to want).
This is a distinction that people don’t always make since it doesn’t impact the sentence structure or conjugation in anyway, but I wanted to explain why it might appear that we are “breaking the rule”. Let’s see an example:

I want a cat.

Would translate to:

Ich will eine Katze.

We are using the modal verb wollen, but we are using it in a non-modal context, because we are expressing our desire for an object (a cat), rather than an action. In this example, it is simply being used as a normal verb, not a modal verb.






NoteTake-home message



Modal verbs are verbs that combine with an infinitive and show the speaker’s attitude towards an action (ability, permission, obligation, desire, etc.). The six modal verbs in German are dürfen, können, mögen, müssen, sollen and wollen.
Sometimes these verbs (usually mögen and wollen) can also be used in a non-modal context when they are expressing a desire for an object. Nothing changes in the sentence structure or conjugation. They aren’t technically acting as modal verbs in those contexts, but rather as normal verbs.








NoteWhat about möchten?



If you have studied a bit of German prior to this, you might be familiar with the verb möchten, which translates to “would like to”. It is often taught early and alongside these modal verbs, because it is commonly used in everyday speech. For example:

I would like to drink something.

Would translate to:

Ich möchte etwas trinken.

You can see that this would be a VERY important verb to know if you were planning a trip to a German-speaking country. It can add a layer of politeness to a request that would not otherwise exist in the sentence:

I want to drink something.

Translated to:

Ich will etwas trinken.

So, if we think about our rules for a modal verb as a helper verb that is showing the speaker’s attitude towards an action (wanting to drink in this example), then möchten is certainly doing that. So why isn’t möchten in our list of modal verbs? Because it already is. The verb möchten is actually a different mood of mögen. For those grammar fans out there, möchten is the subjunctive version of mögen.
At this point, I am not going to discuss the subjunctive, other than to say you should learn to use möchten when politely asking for things or actions. It is the correct form for doing so, but technically, it is a different mood of mögen. It behaves just like the modal verbs discussed (because it is one, mögen).

title: Modal Verbs
text:
The additional verb is implied
Sometimes we don’t use an additional verb in a sentence with a modal verb, because the additional verb is implied from the context. This idea exists in English as well:

Do you have to work?
Yes, I have to.

In this case, we see from the context of the sentence that “Yes, I have to.” is referring to having to work. Without that context, this sentence doesn’t really make sense in English. Additionally, this sentence is just abbreviating the longer sentence “Yes, I have to work.”.






NoteThis process has a special name



The additional verbs in these sentences are elliptically omitted. This means that these words are absent, but the reader/listener can understand and “fill-in” given the context.


This same idea exists in German as well and we can use that same example sentence to demonstrate it:

Musst du arbeiten?
Ja, ich muss.

In this case, “arbeiten” is implied from the context and so it doesn’t need to be repeated, but, like in English, you could choose to include it.

title: Numbers
text:
Counting by hundreds
In order to learn how to count in the hundreds, let’s first talk about counting by hundreds. To count by hundreds, it is just the base number followed by -hundert 🔊.






NoteBrief discussion of 100



One-hundred is a bit different in that it is not “einshundert”, but rather either “einhundert” 🔊 (without the “s”) or simply “hundert” 🔊.

text:
Counting numbers in the hundreds
With our knowledge of the hundreds, tens and ones, we can put them all together. First, we state our hundreds number, followed by the number as it would be formed in 1-99. So 281, would be zweihunderteinundachtzig. As you can imagine, this can start to make some very long words!

text:
Counting the thousands and beyond
Counting in the thousands is just like the counting we have done previously, but we just append -tausend 🔊 to the end of the number. Otherwise, the counting follows the same structure as counting in English. Note that similarly to hundreds, that one thousand is either eintausend 🔊 (without the “s”) or just tausend 🔊. Separators
In the table above, you likely noticed that I have used a “.” rather than a “,” as the thousands separator. That is because, in much of Europe and other places, the “.” is used as the group separator (thousands, millions, billions, etc.) and the “,” is the decimal separator.
When saying a number, Germans will not pronounce that “.” in the same way we don’t pronounce commas in numbers. For decimals, instead of saying “point” like we would say in English, they say Komma 🔊.


One-hundred thousand and beyond
The numbers one-hundred thousand and beyond follow the same conventions that you would expect from English. The table below outlines some numbers beyond ten-thousand.

title: Numbers
text:
Counting form 13 to 19
Now that we have mastered one through twelve, let’s briefly discuss the “teens”. With our knowledge of three through nine, we can now “add zehn” to those numbers to make our teens. To do this, we are going to append -zehn (10) to then end of the numbers to make our “teens”. For example, 13 would turn into dreizehn, or literally translated to “three ten”.






NoteTwo small exceptions to the “add zehn” pattern



In English, the pattern of adding “-teen” to a base number mostly holds, fourteen, sixteen, seventeen, etc., but we have two exceptions to this pattern thirteen and fifteen. They are clearly related to the base number, but they’ve been tweaked a bit.
This same thing happens in German, but it is for the numbers 16 and 17. Instead of “sechszehn” and “siebenzehn”, German uses sechzehn and siebzehn, respectively. As you can see, like in English, the base number is clearly related to its “-teen”/“-zehn” version.

title: Numbers
text:
Counting by tens
The next step is to learn the tens. In English, we often add -ty to the base number to denote it’s in the tens place (fourty, sixty, seventy, ninety). This same idea happens in German but it is with -zig.
English has three exceptions to this pattern (twenty, thirty and fifty are not called “twoty”, “threety” and “fivety”) and so does German. In German, the numbers that don’t quite fit the pattern are 20, 60 and 70 and they are translated to zwanzig, sechzig and siebzig, respectively. Similarly to adding “-zehn” for the “-teens”, sechs gets shortened to sech- and sieben gets shortened to sieb-.

title: Numbers
text:
Counting from 20 to 99
Armed with how to count by ones and tens, we can count from 20 to 99 with no issues. In English, we say twenty-one, twenty-two, twenty-three, etc., where there is the tens place number followed by the ones place number and separated by a dash.
German has a similar concept, but it feels a bit wonky (at least to me). German states the ones place number first, then the tens place number and connects them with und, which means “and” in German. So twenty-two translates to zweiundzwanzig. This would literally translate to “two and twenty”. The one exception we need to be aware of is that eins (one) turns to ein-, so “twenty-one” translates to einundzwanzig rather than “einsundzwanzig”. title: Shapes
text: Approximate time: 30 minutes

title: Shapes
text:
Polygons
Polygons in German are a little easier than in English, but it generally follows a similar pattern. As you increase the number of sides in English after a quadrilateral, you simply have a numerical prefix (penta-, hexa-, etc.) followed by -gon. The etymology of -gon in reference to shapes traces back to the Greek suffix -gōnia (-γωνο), which means angles. So something like pentagon can be broken down into “five angles”/“five corners”.
In German, like in English, they count the corners, but instead of waiting until pentagon to adopt the numerical prefix + -corner convention, they start it at triangle. The German word for corner is generally die Ecke, but das Eck is another, less common way, to say corner. You may hear das Eck in Southern Germany, Austria and parts of Switzerland. It is also used to count the corners of polygons in German though. The table below to see the pattern for counting the size of a polygon in German:



English
German
English
German

quadrilateral

hexagon

octagon

das Zehneck



We can see that it is simply the number of corners in the polygon, followed by -eck suffix. In fact, polygon translates directly to das Vieleck (viel is the German work for many), so the connections between German and English for polygons is pretty strong.

title: Shapes
text:
Special quadrilaterals
While this pattern works for generally describing polygons, there are also some special quadrilaterals in German as well. In the same way that squares, rectangles and parallelograms are all special quadrilaterals in English, there are special names for different quadrilaterals, which can be found in the table below:

title: Shapes
text:
Prisms
Let’s start discussing three-dimensional shapes with the prisms, because they follow a pattern, but it isn’t likely the what you would guess given a background in English. In English, we take the name for the face of the prism and often add “-al prism” (pentagonal prism, hexagonal prism, etc.). Notable triangular and rectangular prisms don’t fit that pattern in English. The good news is that all prisms in German follow the same pattern, but for reason German counts the sides on the face shape rather than the corners for prisms. Of course the number of sides and corners are equal in polygons, so it doesn’t matter if you count sides or corners, but it is a mnemonic that helps be think about shapes in German.
The German word for “side” in relation to shapes is die Seite (notably also means page) and “-sided” is -seitig and “prism” is das Prisma. So let’s see how German constructs prism shapes in the table below:



English
German




triangular prism
das dreiseitige Prisma


quadrilateral prism
das vierseitige Prisma


pentagonal prism
das fünfseitige Prisma


hexagonal prism
das sechseitige Prisma



You will notice that -e is appended to the end of number + seitig. This has to do with “three-sided”, “four-sided”, etc. are adjectives and this is the behavior for adjectives in this context. We are going to talk more about adjectives at a later point, so for now you can just remember to add -e.

title: Shapes
text:
Special shapes
Unfortunately like cube, cone and sphere in English, German has some unique names for some three-dimensional shapes that just need to be memorized. There is no pattern that you can rely on.



English
German




cube
der Würfel


rectangular prism
der Quader*


triangular pyramid
die dreiseitige Pyramide


square pyramid
die vierseitige Pyramide


cylinder
der Zylinder


cone
der Kegel


sphere
die Kugel


hemisphere
die Halbkugel**



* A rectangular prism (der Quader) is a special type of quadrilateral prism (das vierseitiges Prisma), because a prism of a trapezoid would be also be a type of quadrilateral prism, but not a rectangular prism. When describing a rectangular prism most Germans would use der Quader over das vierseitiges Prisma in the same way that English speakers would like use rectangular prism over quadrilateral prism. It sounds a bit geometry textbook-ish to say das vierseitiges Prisma just like quadrilateral prism sounds in English.
** Once again, like Halbkreis, it is useful to know fractions since Halbkugel literally translates to “half sphere”.
